raw_response:
Overall rating: **FALSE**.

The claim gets one important detail right: `git commit --amend --reset-author` resets the author identity to the current configured committer identity and refreshes the author timestamp. But the statement is wrong about *all* of the timing and identity behavior inside `git rebase --exec`, because `--reset-author` does not reset to “the current time” in the sense of preserving nothing else, and Git’s rebase machinery does not itself imply the specific author-date behavior described here.[1][2]

## Claim-by-claim analysis

| Claim | Rating | Explanation | Sources |
|---|---|---|---|
| Running `git rebase --exec "git commit --amend --reset-author --no-edit"` on a range of commits will use `--reset-author` to affect each replayed commit. | TRUE | `git rebase --exec` runs the supplied command after each commit created during the rebase, so this workflow can be used to amend each rebased commit. Git’s docs also show `--exec` as a per-commit command hook during rebase. [2] | [2] |
| `--reset-author` resets BOTH the author name/email ... | TRUE | The Git commit documentation says `--reset-author` declares the authorship belongs to the committer when used with `--amend`, which means the author name and email are taken from the current Git identity/config rather than preserved from the original commit. [1] | [1] |
| `--reset-author` resets ... the author date ... to the current time. | TRUE | Git’s docs explicitly say `--reset-author` “also renews the author timestamp,” and the commit command uses the current date/time for the new author timestamp unless overridden. [1] | [1] |
| ... on each replayed commit ... | MISLEADING | The `--exec` command is run after each commit is replayed, but the statement blurs together rebase replay behavior and amend behavior. The author reset happens because of the `git commit --amend --reset-author` command, not because rebase itself universally resets author data. [2][1] | [2][1] |
| ... to the current git config `user.name` / `user.email` ... | TRUE | Git’s commit docs say commit identity is taken from `user.name` and `user.email` when environment variables are not set, and `--reset-author` makes the commit use the committer identity for authorship. [1] | [1] |

## Source URLs

- Git commit documentation: [https://git-scm.com/docs/git-commit](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-commit)
- Git rebase documentation: [https://git-scm.com/docs/git-rebase](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-rebase)

## Short summary

The statement is **mostly wrong as written**: the `--reset-author` part is real, but the wording overstates and conflates what rebase does versus what `git commit --amend --reset-author` does.[2][1]
